entry.title: Escaping Hugo Shortcodes in Posts
entry.summary:
TL;DR

Add /* after the opening shortcode tags i.e. {{</* and before the closing shortcode tags i.e. */>}}

While creating the post Beautifulhugo Theme Extras - Shortcodes (Split), I had quite a bit of trouble finding the answer to the question of “how can I display a block of code in …
entry.content:
TL;DR

Add /* after the opening shortcode tags i.e. {{</* and before the closing shortcode tags i.e. */>}}

While creating the post Beautifulhugo Theme Extras - Shortcodes (Split), I had quite a bit of trouble finding the answer to the question of “how can I display a block of code in my post?“.

The answer seemed obvious, use a code fence i.e. use triple backticks; OR indent the code block using 4 spaces; OR after adding a colon (:), surround the code by two blank lines, one at the top and one below the last line of code.

As it turned out, that was the wrong question! I, Robot anyone?

The right question was “what is the markdown escape sequence required to correctly display a hugo shortcode in my post i.e. without it being interpreted?“.

The answer eventually came to me via Desmond Lua at LuaSoftware.

Thanks Desmond! 😅

entry.title: Beautifulhugo Theme Extras - Shortcodes (Split)
entry.summary: Another extra beautifulhugo theme shortcode is called split. This shortcode splits the page into two columns. The example shown in the doco in the beautifulhugo repo (at the time of writing) was a little confusing because the closing endcolumns tag was incorrect (missing an s). Got there in the end …
entry.content:
Another extra beautifulhugo theme shortcode is called split. This shortcode splits the page into two columns. The example shown in the doco in the beautifulhugo repo (at the time of writing) was a little confusing because the closing endcolumns tag was incorrect (missing an s). Got there in the end. 😅

It should look like this:

{{</* columns */>}}
    First column on the right side of the page
{{</* column */>}}
    Second column on the left side of the page
{{</* endcolumns */>}}

Yes, the first tag is columns whereas the intermediate tag is column. And the closing tag is again a plural i.e. endcolumns.

entry.title: Beautifulhugo Theme Extras - Shortcodes (Details)
entry.summary:
An extra beautifulhugo theme shortcode is called details. This shortcode hides content until the text set on the detail is clicked.

The example code looks like this:

{{</* details "Click here to expand to show the content!" */>}}
<br>
   This is the content you'd like to hide.
<br …
entry.content:
An extra beautifulhugo theme shortcode is called details. This shortcode hides content until the text set on the detail is clicked.

The example code looks like this:

{{</* details "Click here to expand to show the content!" */>}}
<br>
   This is the content you'd like to hide.
<br>
   🧘‍♂️
{{</* /details */>}}

I was having trouble adding spacing to the hidden content, above and below “This is the content you’d like to hide.” in the example above so I resorted to adding HTML <br> tags. The markdown linter I have installed doesn’t like it though - no inline HTML rule broken. Still works.

And I had to add numerous non-breaking spaces to push the content to the right i.e.     - there must be a different way!?

Curiously also, I found that it is impossible to add extra whitespace after the details shortcode. I’ve tried adding 1 more more blank lines inside of <pre></pre> tags and I’ve added numerous <br> tags after the details shortcode but neither of these approaches worked.

The learning continues! 🏃‍♂

entry.title: Beautifulhugo Theme Extras - Chroma Server Side Syntax Highlighting
entry.summary:
The beautifulhugo theme has support for Hugo’s Chroma (a general purpose syntax highlighter in pure Go) syntax highlighting.

Let me try that out! 🚀

Add the following to the site config i.e.config.toml file:

pygmentsCodeFences = true
pygmentsUseClasses = true

Then from the command line, generate the desired new code …
entry.content:
The beautifulhugo theme has support for Hugo’s Chroma (a general purpose syntax highlighter in pure Go) syntax highlighting.

Let me try that out! 🚀

Add the following to the site config i.e.config.toml file:

pygmentsCodeFences = true
pygmentsUseClasses = true

Then from the command line, generate the desired new code style (in my case, monokai) by running:

hugo gen chromastyles --style=monokai > static/syntax.css

Or if you want to add more structure to your static files, add the following to the site config i.e. config.toml file:

staticDir = ["static", "static/css"]

And then re-generate the code style using the new folder path i.e. static/css :

hugo gen chromastyles --style=monokai > static/css/syntax.css
